system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 9: Scale Runs, Minor/Dominant/Diminished Runs and Blues Pattern (Lessons 27-31)

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 9 — Scale Runs, Minor/Dominant/Diminished Runs and Blues Pattern (Lessons 27-31)

_Pages 30-34._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Chromatic fingering invariance

> *You are to use the same fingering, up and down the fingerboard.

**Significance (p. 30).** States the load-bearing position-system rule: a single fingering shape for a run is transposed by simple position-shift, which is how Baker turns one learned pattern into a 12-key vocabulary.

## Same fingering all directions

> use the same fingering going up and down the fingerboard.

**Significance (p. 31).** Reiterates the invariant-fingering doctrine for dominant 7th/9th runs — the mechanical key to executing Baker's chromatic transposition drill at tempo.

## Mastery prerequisite for blues

> Now that you have your key runs in your head, — of course, I know that you haven’t mastered them yet, but you
>
> should at least know where to find any run that I tell you.
> your guitar, you’re not ready for this lesson.

**Significance (p. 32).** Sets the gating criterion before the blues lessons: locate any named run anywhere on the neck on demand. Without this recall the blues solos that follow can't be executed.

## Blues centrality in jazz

> The Biues play a very important part in modern jazz, so we are going to start our solo work with blues patterns.

**Significance (p. 32).** Declares blues as the entry vehicle for all solo work in the method, motivating why the next several lessons are blues-form-centric.

## Five blues keys

> The keys that the blues are played in mostly, are: —F, G, A’, BS and C. So these are the keys that you are to
> concentrate on for now. These simple patterns that I have written out in the next few lessons are to be trans-
> posed to all five of these keys.

**Significance (p. 32).** Identifies the five horn-friendly blues keys that constrain the transposition drill for the blues solos, narrowing the all-keys mandate to a practical subset for this section.

## Reuse of exercise runs

> Now I am using these runs just as they were in the exercise, so all you have to
> do is to find the right one.

**Significance (p. 32).** Confirms that the blues solos are built from the exact runs of Lessons 27-30 with no modification, which is how the exercise vocabulary directly becomes improvisational material.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
